name: streaming-partial-writes
description: Claude streams a multi-sentence prose response across several JSONL chunks. The parser must keep the session in working across all intermediate assistant_streaming events (non-terminal stop_reason) and only flip to ready when the terminal end_turn event lands. This locks down the Bug-D streaming-flicker family (issue #102): resolveAssistantStreaming (parser.go:288) downgrades non-terminal stop_reasons to assistant_streaming, which is excluded from IsAgentDone(). The streaming_window phase pins the no-flicker invariant.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Primitive

The scenario exercises `streaming_output` — the agent emits assistant content across multiple partial flushes before a final turn-end signal, and the irrlicht invariant is that those partial flushes must NOT trigger a premature `→ ready` (no `ready → working → ready → working` flicker). The canonical signal is `ready → working → ready` with state pinned at `working` across every flush. The matching `replaydata/agents/aider/capabilities.json` key is `streaming_output` (= true).

## agent_supports: yes

Aider supports streaming output. `capabilities.json` records `streaming_output: true`, and aider's docs confirm `--stream` (streaming pretty LLM responses) is enabled by default and only auto-disabled for models that lack support. In aider's transcript shape the relevant 'partial flush' unit is per-model-call: under `--yes-always`, a single `####` user turn drives multiple model calls (e.g. an initial reply, then auto-accepted file-adds, then a follow-up analysis), each delimited by a `> Tokens: …` line. The committed regression transcript `replaydata/agents/aider/regression/yes-always-multi-step/transcript.md` shows exactly this: two prose blocks (lines 12–20 and 30–34), each terminated by its own `> Tokens:` line within one `####` turn.

## daemon_capability: full

The aider adapter is `FilesUnderCWD` + `RawLineParser` over `.aider.chat.history.md` (`agent.go:45-51`). The parser buffers plain prose lines silently (`parser.go:137-143`, returns nil) and emits `assistant_message` — NOT `turn_done` — at each `> Tokens:` line (`closeModelCall`, `parser.go:153-188`), keeping `turnOpen` true across model calls (verified by `TestParser_MultiModelCall_KeepsTurnOpen`). `IsAgentDone()` (`session.go:461-496`) treats only `turn_done` (and the Claude-specific `assistant`/`assistant_output` fallback) as done — aider's `assistant_message` is NOT matched, so per-call flushes cannot flip the session to `ready`. The session holds `working` across every back-to-back flush, which is exactly the no-flicker invariant the scenario pins. For the common case the daemon emits the lifecycle events the spec requires (`ready → working`, no intermediate `→ ready`, then a single `working → ready` at turn end), so the daemon axis is `full`.

The spec invariant — "partial flushes must NOT flip the session to ready mid-turn" — is met by the parser as it exists today for completed per-model-call prose blocks. aider has no token-level streaming trace at all (terminal streaming is TUI-only; the markdown history is written as completed message blocks — confirmed against aider's `--stream` / `--chat-history-file` docs), so there is no spec-required streaming-chunk event for the daemon to mis-handle. There is no recorded `events.jsonl` line showing the daemon mis-parsing a real trace, so this is NOT a `bug`; the residual timing hazard is captured as a caveat (below) rather than a downgrade.

## driver_capability: ready

The recipe needs only `send`, `wait_turn` (×2, to span a genuine multi-model-call turn driven by the `--auto-test --test-cmd false` reflection loop), and `sleep` — all implemented by `scripts/drive-aider-interactive.sh`. No gapped primitive (restart / reset_session / resume / start_session / exit_clean / sigkill / keys / session / fork) is required, so the driver is `ready`.

## Why not daemon_capability: bug

The one carve-out is timing, not mis-handling of a trace: aider's only clean end-of-turn signal is `IdleFlush`, which synthesizes `turn_done` after the history is quiet for `aiderIdleTurnDoneAfter` = 1500ms (`parser.go:54`, `216-229`). A slow remote model call that leaves the file quiet >1500ms BETWEEN flushes can make IdleFlush synthesize a premature `turn_done` → premature `→ ready` → flicker. But this is a timing-window artifact of the IdleFlush heuristic for non-spec-representable token streaming, not the daemon mis-parsing a trace it should handle; no cited `events.jsonl` line demonstrates a real mis-handling. Per the authoring rule (caveats over downgrades; reserve `bug` for a spec-required observable the daemon mis-handles), this stays `daemon_capability: full` with the hazard recorded as a caveat. The recipe targets the no-flicker IDEAL and the `streaming_window` invariant in a recording is what would surface any such premature-ready drift.

## Matrix

The coverage row already carries the two-axis schema (`agent_supports: yes`, `daemon_capability: unknown`, `driver_capability: ready`). This assessment resolves the `unknown` daemon axis to `full`; the maintainer should update the coverage row's `daemon_capability` from `unknown` → `full`.
caveats: metric-drift-downstream: aider's end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1500ms of transcript quiet (parser.go:54, 216-229); a slow remote model call that goes quiet >1500ms BETWEEN flushes can fire a premature turn_done → premature ready, flickering the no-flicker invariant the scenario pins. Holds for normal back-to-back flushes (the common case) but is not airtight against a slow remote call.

feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant: the scenario's canonical text describes Claude/JSONL assistant_streaming chunks (parser.go:288, non-terminal stop_reason); aider has no equivalent JSONL streaming event — its 'partial flush' unit is the per-model-call prose block terminated by a `> Tokens:` line, so the recipe must drive multi-model-call prose within one turn (via the --auto-test reflection loop), not token-level streaming.

sources.note: streaming_output: true — confirms the primitive-exercised capability key

closeModelCall emits assistant_message (not turn_done) per `> Tokens:` line; turnOpen stays true; IdleFlush (aiderIdleTurnDoneAfter=1500ms) is the only clean end-of-turn signal (lines 54, 153-188, 216-229)

IsAgentDone (461-496): only turn_done (or Claude's assistant/assistant_output fallback) is 'done'; aider's assistant_message is NOT matched, so per-call flushes keep the session working — the no-flicker invariant holds for back-to-back flushes (daemon_capability: full)
TestParser_MultiModelCall_KeepsTurnOpen + TestParser_IdleFlush_AfterMultipleModelCalls_EmitsTurnDone: turn stays open across multiple `> Tokens:` lines, closes only on idle flush
real transcript: two prose blocks within one #### turn, each terminated by its own `> Tokens:` line — the aider analog of partial flushes
--stream enabled by default (TUI streaming); --chat-history-file defaults to .aider.chat.history.md — file is written as completed message blocks, not token-by-token
aider streaming behaviour and chat-history markdown logging

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a model out-of-band (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL plus the matching provider key/endpoint — e.g. a local LM Studio / OpenAI-compatible server). Headless aider with no reachable provider hangs on the model-auth prompt; the live REPL needs a model that actually answers the prompt and streams a multi-part reply.
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so the streamed turn is observed across its partial flushes while the process stays alive
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

OBSERVES:PARTIAL CARVE-OUT (mirror of the coverage note): aider's end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1500ms of transcript quiet (parser.go:54); a SLOW remote model call that leaves the history file quiet >1500ms BETWEEN flushes (mid-turn) can fire a premature turn_done → premature working→ready, flickering the no-flicker invariant this cell pins. The no-flicker property holds for normal back-to-back flushes (the common case) but is NOT airtight against a slow remote call; this recipe targets the no-flicker IDEAL, and the streaming_window invariant is what surfaces any such premature-ready drift in a recording.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in the transcript before sending the prompt

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears. aider is keyed by proc-<PID> — there is no transcript UUID rollout session; the whole arc stays on the one proc-<PID> row

AIDER'S PARTIAL-FLUSH UNIT IS THE PER-MODEL-CALL PROSE BLOCK, NOT TOKEN-LEVEL STREAMING: aider has no JSONL streaming event and the markdown history is written as completed message blocks (incremental token rendering is terminal-UI only and never reaches the file). The partial-flush analog is the per-model-CALL prose block closed by its own `> Tokens:` line. closeModelCall (parser.go:153-188) emits assistant_message — NOT turn_done — at each `> Tokens:` line and leaves the turn open, and IsAgentDone (session.go:461-496) does not treat aider's assistant_message as done, so no per-call flush can flip the session to ready: the session holds working across every partial flush (TestParser_MultiModelCall_KeepsTurnOpen)

ELICITING A MULTI-MODEL-CALL TURN DETERMINISTICALLY (the fix for the single-flush failure mode): a plain reply prompt — even a long 'walk through these files' one — can be answered by the model in ONE model call (one `> Tokens:` flush), so there are NO intermediate partial flushes to observe and the streaming_window has nothing to assert (the prior recording reproduced exactly this: one `> Tokens:` line, then a late --yes-always file-write reopened working AFTER the turn settled, exiting while working). Instead, borrow the aider/self-correction-iteration mechanism: settings.aider_extra_args launches aider with `--auto-test --test-cmd false`. The send asks aider to create one small Python file (an edit it can reliably perform); `false` always exits non-zero, so EVERY edit trips a test failure and forces a reflection re-call within the SAME `#### ` turn (run_one's max_reflections=3 loop, base_coder.py:860/879/924-944, never re-emits a `#### ` header). Each reflection round closes its own `> Tokens:` line, so one user turn deterministically produces ≥2 model calls — a genuine multi-flush span the session must hold working across, independent of what gpt-5-nano writes. The loop self-limits at max_reflections=3 so the turn always settles to ready; no permission gate (--yes-always, permission_hooks:false) so the edit + auto-test auto-execute and the session never enters waiting.

wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears (one model call finished). The recipe waits TWICE: the FIRST wait_turn returns on the initial edit's `> Tokens:`, the SECOND returns on the first reflection round's `> Tokens:` (the auto-test failed and aider re-called the model) — this guarantees a genuine multi-call streaming span completed before teardown; a single wait_turn returns while the reflection call is still in flight and truncates the turn. aider has NO turn-end transcript marker — end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1.5s quiet (parser.go:54,216-229), surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's 5s SessionDetector poll (~5s effective latency); the trailing sleep (≥10s; here 20s) absorbs all reflection rounds (max_reflections=3 — gpt-5-nano needs >12s to complete them, so 12s teardown-interrupted an in-flight third round) plus one full ~5s IdleFlush cycle so the final working→ready settle fires AND is observed before teardown — a teardown race bit a sibling cell at 8s, so the final settle + teardown needs ≥10s of headroom before the tmux kill
After the trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the session

script.text: Create a new file solution.py containing a single Python function add(a, b) that returns a + b. Make the edit now; do not ask me anything.

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession (proc-<PID>) event in ready within ~2s of REPL launch (aider's transcript is lazy, so the pre-session is the launch-time observable)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the live aider session_id
events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working transition for the session when the prompt lands — the streaming window opens here

across the streaming window (the --auto-test reflection loop drives MULTIPLE model calls within the one `####` turn — each per-model-call prose block closed by its own `> Tokens:` line emits assistant_message, not turn_done), NO state_transition to ready fires before the synthesized end-of-turn; the session holds working solidly across all flushes (the no-flicker invariant)
NO state_transition to waiting fires during the streaming window
events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition at the synthesized turn end (IdleFlush after the history goes quiet, ~5s effective latency)
the session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the session at REPL teardown (one long-lived session)
transcript .aider.chat.history.md contains 1 `####` user line and at least 2 `> Tokens:` lines (the initial edit plus ≥1 reflection re-edit — the multiple partial flushes within the one turn)

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

Claude Code natively streams assistant output in chunks: every assistant JSONL line carries a `stop_reason` that is either nil/non-terminal while the turn is still in flight, or one of the terminal values (`end_turn`, `stop_sequence`, `refusal`, `tool_use`) when the turn finishes. The transcript file is append-only, so the tailer naturally sees the deltas land one line at a time.

The spec's only assertion is that state stays `working` across all partial flushes and does NOT flicker to `ready` before the turn's terminal line. irrlicht's claudecode parser handles this explicitly in `resolveAssistantStreaming` (parser.go:288): any assistant event whose `stop_reason` is not on the terminal allow-list is downgraded to the `assistant_streaming` event type, which is excluded from `IsAgentDone()`. The allow-list approach is deliberately defensive — unknown future stop reasons default to 'assume streaming' (the safe side of the Bug-D flicker, issue #102).

Furthermore, the parser exposes `PendingContribution()` (parser.go:485) and implements `PendingContributor`, so the tailer surfaces the in-progress turn's cost during streaming without committing it as a completed contribution. The request-ID-scoped dedup in `applyRequestIDContribution` (parser.go:260) keeps multiple streaming events with the same `requestId` collapsed into one contribution that is only flushed when the next `requestId` (i.e. next turn) arrives.

Observability ladder: (1) every partial flush appears as a `transcript_activity` event but does NOT promote to a state transition; (2) state band stays solid `working` across the chunks; (3) the terminal `end_turn` line (or a follow-up `turn_done` system event) lands the single `working → ready` transition.
caveats: Recording this is mostly a question of provoking a verbose enough response. A long prose answer (e.g. 'write a 500-word essay about X') reliably emits several intermediate assistant events with non-terminal stop_reason. The recipe wouldn't add new step types — it's a vanilla send/wait, but the phase contract should assert an invariant `no state_transition_to_ready_before_turn_done` across the streaming window.

Unknown future stop_reason values are mapped to streaming (allow-list default). If Anthropic introduces a new terminal stop_reason without updating the allow-list, the turn would appear to stick in `working` until the next user input — a known trade-off documented in the resolveAssistantStreaming comment.

sources.note: resolveAssistantStreaming (line 288) — non-terminal stop_reason downgrades to assistant_streaming, excluded from IsAgentDone(). Terminal allow-list: end_turn, stop_sequence, refusal, tool_use.
applyRequestIDContribution (line 260) and PendingContribution (line 485) — request-ID-scoped dedup keeps streaming chunks from emitting duplicate completed contributions.
Bug D in the streaming-flicker family — drove the resolveAssistantStreaming allow-list approach so unknown stop_reasons default to 'assume streaming'.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/

script.text: Write a 5-sentence story about a robot. Each sentence on its own line.

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready after launch
events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working transition for the UUID
across the streaming window (multiple assistant_streaming events with non-terminal stop_reason), NO state_transition to ready fires before the terminal end_turn line
NO state_transition to waiting fires during the streaming window
events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition at turn end
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains 1 user line and at least 2 assistant lines (intermediate streaming chunks + terminal)

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: incapable; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: incapable; driver_capability: ready

applicable: n/a — frozen. The scenario's invariant cannot be exercised by a live codex recording; it is a parser-agnostic tailer-robustness property already covered by synthetic unit tests.

## What the scenario pins

`scenario-meanings.md` defines `streaming-partial-writes` by the `streaming_output` primitive: the assistant message arrives in multiple partial flushes before the turn-end signal, and state must stay `working` across all flushes with no premature `→ ready`. The canonical claudecode recipe (the row template) maps this onto a very specific mechanism: claudecode writes multiple intermediate assistant JSONL lines carrying NON-TERMINAL `stop_reason` values, and `resolveAssistantStreaming` (claudecode parser.go:288) downgrades those to `assistant_streaming`, which `IsAgentDone()` excludes. The `streaming_window` phase asserts no `→ ready` flicker fires across that window until the terminal `end_turn` line lands.

## Why codex can't reproduce the live signal

Codex's rollout transcript model is structurally different. Inspecting a real session (`~/.codex/sessions/2026/05/23/rollout-…019e568a….jsonl`):

- Assistant content is written as `response_item` / `payload.type == "message"` / `role == "assistant"` records whose payload keys are exactly `content, phase, role, type` — there is NO `stop_reason` field, and zero occurrences of `assistant_streaming`, `"stop_reason":"null"`, or `"partial":true` anywhere in the file.
- Each assistant `message` line is appended atomically as one complete JSONL event. There is no non-terminal-stop-reason streaming-chunk family for the parser to downgrade.
- The codex parser (parser.go) has no `assistant_streaming` concept at all. The `working → ready` flip is driven by the discrete `event_msg` / `payload.type == "task_complete"` signal, which parser.go:166-169 maps to `turn_done` (the comment there explicitly notes this exists to AVOID working→ready→working flicker on intermediate assistant messages). So codex has no `resolveAssistantStreaming` invariant to pin.

A true "partial write" — assistant bytes flushed mid-line with no trailing `\n` — is a byte-level filesystem-timing artifact. An interactive driver (`tmux send-keys` + transcript tail) cannot deterministically force codex to leave a JSONL line half-written at the moment the daemon ticks; codex flushes whole append-only lines. Forcing it would be a contrived recording that does not actually test the property.

## Where the real property lives (and is already tested)

Tolerating a partial trailing line is the SHARED tailer's job, not codex-specific. `core/pkg/tailer/tailer.go:414-426` reads with a `bufio.Reader` and, on `errPartialAtEOF`, stops WITHOUT advancing the offset past the partial bytes, so they are re-read next tick once the writer flushes the rest. This is locked down by synthetic unit tests in `core/pkg/tailer/large_line_test.go`: `TestTailAndProcess_PartialTrailingLine` (line 73) writes a complete line followed by partial bytes of an assistant event with no `\n`, asserts the offset does not advance past the partial bytes, then appends the remainder and confirms the line is parsed and the session settles. `TestReadLineCapped_PartialAtEOF` (line 171) covers the same at the reader layer.

Because this invariant is (a) parser-agnostic, (b) a byte-timing artifact a live driver can't deterministically force, and (c) already covered by deterministic synthetic fixtures, a live codex recording adds no fidelity. `daemon_capability: incapable; driver_capability: ready` — there is no codex-specific lifecycle event that would prove the partial-write invariant, and the daemon's robustness here is exercised by the tailer unit tests, not by a per-adapter recording.

## Conclusion

Cell frozen at `applicable: n/a`. No recipe, no recording. To unblock would require codex to ship an observable non-terminal streaming-chunk family in its rollout JSONL (none today), or a reframing of the scenario from "live streaming flicker" to "tailer partial-line robustness" — the latter is already green under `go test ./core/pkg/tailer/...`.
caveats: agent_supports is partial, not yes: codex does stream output to the terminal, but the on-disk rollout transcript records whole assistant message lines atomically with no non-terminal stop_reason / streaming-chunk family — so the specific resolveAssistantStreaming invariant this scenario pins has no codex analogue to observe.

The partial-write tolerance the scenario cares about is a shared-tailer property (tailer.go errPartialAtEOF path) covered by synthetic unit tests (large_line_test.go TestTailAndProcess_PartialTrailingLine), not a codex-recordable behavior; a live driver cannot deterministically force a mid-line flush.

sources.note: streaming-partial-writes definition: streaming_output primitive, no premature →ready across partial flushes.
No assistant_streaming / stop_reason handling; turn-end flip driven by task_complete→turn_done (lines 155-170). Comment notes this exists to avoid working→ready→working flicker.
Source is FilesUnderRoot{Dir: sessionsDir(), Parser: JSONLineParser} — append-only rollout JSONL under ~/.codex/sessions.
Lines 414-426: bufio.Reader; on errPartialAtEOF stops without advancing offset past partial bytes, re-read next tick. This is the actual partial-write robustness mechanism, parser-agnostic.
TestTailAndProcess_PartialTrailingLine (line 73) and TestReadLineCapped_PartialAtEOF (line 171): synthetic unit tests that already lock down partial-line tolerance and re-read-once-flushed settling.

Real codex session: assistant message payload keys are content/phase/role/type only (no stop_reason); zero occurrences of assistant_streaming / partial flags; one complete assistant line appended per turn segment, terminated by task_complete.

assessment.notes: opencode streams assistant output as incremental text parts; parseTextPart maps assistant text to assistant_message (no turn boundary, excluded from IsAgentDone) and step-finish reason=tool-calls also stays working — only step-finish reason=stop emits turn_done. The WAL-fsnotify ProcessOwnedStore watcher observes the SQLite part rows for the ses_… session, so partial flushes that reach the WAL across multiple scan windows do not flicker to ready. Driver: a single send producing a longer multi-line reply (opencode run blocks; wait_turn no-op). OBSERVABILITY RISK to confirm at record time: a no-tool prose turn run headlessly can flush all assistant text + the terminal step-finish to the WAL in one scan window (basic-turn collapsed working→ready in ~0.3ms), giving the streaming_window nothing intermediate to assert; if so this is an observability gap to surface, not a spec rebase.

sources.excerpt: parseTextPart assistant -> assistant_message (no turn boundary); only step-finish reason=stop -> turn_done
WAL-fsnotify-driven scanParts: part rows with time_updated >= cursor emitted as EventActivity

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its default model (run a single message in the opencode TUI to confirm; the default is a local LM Studio model so no remote key is needed)
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

OBSERVABILITY CAVEAT (mirror of the spec's notes): opencode persists session parts to SQLite, observed by the daemon's WAL-fsnotify-driven ProcessOwnedStore watcher (scanParts, time_updated cursor). A no-tool prose turn run headlessly via blocking `opencode run` can flush its assistant text part(s) AND the terminal step-finish reason=stop to the WAL inside one scan window at turn completion — basic-turn collapsed working→ready in ~0.3ms for exactly this reason. The multi-line prompt aims to spread the assistant text across several WAL flushes so the working dwell is genuinely observable; but if opencode writes the whole reply atomically the streaming_window has nothing intermediate to assert over. If a live recording confirms the atomic-write behavior, that is an inherent ProcessOwnedStore observability gap (surface it as observability_correction → daemon_capability:incapable), NOT a flaky recipe and NOT a reason to rebase expected.jsonl.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) creates a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` for the single send (no --model flag, so opencode uses its configured default model); the run blocks until the assistant turn finishes, so wait_turn is a no-op

OpenCode session state lives in its SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), not per-cwd transcript files; the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the part table (WAL-fsnotify-driven, debounced by minScanGap) and observes the UUID-keyed ses_… session for this cwd. The driver discovers that session id from SQLite after the run returns (opencode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs)

OPENCODE'S STREAMED UNIT IS THE ASSISTANT TEXT PART: parseTextPart maps every assistant text part to assistant_message (parser.go:178-197 — no turn boundary, excluded from IsAgentDone), and step-finish reason=tool-calls also maps to assistant_message (parser.go:155-157). Only step-finish reason=stop emits turn_done (parser.go:152-154) and settles the turn working→ready. So no intermediate part flush can flip the session to ready: the session holds working across every text/non-stop flush that reaches the WAL — the no-flicker invariant the streaming_window pins

ELICITING A MULTI-FLUSH WORKING DWELL: the send asks for a moderately long multi-line prose answer (a short numbered list, one item per line) so the assistant emits its content as several text parts that flush to the WAL across multiple scan windows, giving the daemon an observable working dwell BEFORE the terminal step-finish reason=stop. This is the no-tool analog of opencode/self-correction-iteration's multi-step turn — but with tool_calls_max:0 there is no permission gate, so the session never enters waiting

The default model is a small, slow local LM Studio model (~30–45s per turn), so the trailing sleep and timeout are generous: a slow multi-line turn must not trip a false timeout, and the trailing sleep must outlast the turn_done settle so the step-finish-driven working→ready is observed before teardown

script.text: Write a 5-step guide for making a cup of tea. Number each step 1 through 5, and put each step on its own line. Write at least one full sentence per step.

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new (or session_created) for the ses_… session, born ready, within ~2s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear; the turn arc and pid_discovered land on the ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id
events.jsonl shows exactly one state_transition→working on the ses_… session_id when the multi-line prompt is sent — the streaming window opens here

across the streaming window (opencode emits the assistant content as incremental text parts, each mapped to assistant_message — non-terminal), NO state_transition to ready fires before the terminal step-finish reason=stop; the session holds working solidly across all flushes (the no-flicker invariant)
NO state_transition to waiting fires during the streaming window (tool_calls_max:0 — no tool call, no permission gate)
events.jsonl shows exactly one state_transition→ready on the same ses_… session_id at turn end (step-finish reason=stop → turn_done closes the turn)
the session's final live state is ready

transcript.jsonl contains 1 user line and at least 1 assistant text line plus the terminal step-finish (if the reply lands as multiple text parts, multiple assistant lines appear — the observable multi-flush span)

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: incapable — pi writes completed messages atomically; no partial-flush trace in the rollout the daemon tails
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict (reconciled 2026-05-28)

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: incapable — pi writes completed messages atomically; no partial-flush trace in the rollout the daemon tails
driver_capability: ready

The prior daemon_capability:full was a code-read inference assuming pi flushes partial assistant content to the JSONL as it streams. A live recording disproved the premise: a multi-line reply lands as a single stopReason=stop assistant line, and sibling pi transcripts (basic-turn, multi-turn-conversation) show one consolidated line per turn. Streamed partial content lives only in the terminal UI, which the daemon never sees.

---

## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

`streaming-partial-writes` (scenario-meanings.md): the assistant message arrives in multiple partial flushes before the turn-end signal; state must stay `working` across all flushes with no premature `→ ready`. User-observable signal: `ready → working → ready`, with `working` sustained across every partial flush — no flicker to `ready` before the turn-end signal arrives. Primitive `streaming_output`; the irrlicht primitive is that partial flushes do NOT trigger a `→ ready` transition. Requires `headless_mode`.

**agent_supports: yes.** Pi streams assistant output incrementally and writes assistant content to the JSONL rollout as it goes. A prompt that elicits a longer reply (e.g. a multi-line haiku or a numbered list of facts) produces several assistant transcript writes within one turn before the final assistant message lands.

**daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready.** Pi's turn boundary is defined ONLY by `stopReason == "stop"`. The pi parser (parser.go:78-83) maps an assistant message with `stopReason == "stop"` to `turn_done` (the working→ready settle) and EVERY other assistant message — including non-terminal partial/streamed flushes — to `assistant` (mid-turn), which sustains `working` and is excluded from IsAgentDone(). So streamed/partial assistant writes can never cause a spurious `→ ready` flap: the turn is one contiguous `working → ready` arc. The compaction-interspersing risk noted in the stale coverage cell (`daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready`) does NOT apply: pi emits `compaction` as a top-level event that the parser maps to `assistant` (parser.go:55-60), which ALSO sustains `working` (promotes ready→working) rather than settling to ready — so even a mid-stream compaction cannot flicker the state. That is why this reassessment lifts the verdict to yes/yes.

**Confidence 0.72** (not higher): you cannot deterministically force a torn/partial JSONL line from a live CLI — the assertion here is about state STABILITY across streamed content (one contiguous working→ready arc, no intermediate ready/working flap), not a hand-crafted oversized/torn line (that is the separate oversized-transcript-line cell). The prompt pins a longer reply so streaming clearly happens, but the exact number of intermediate flushes is model-dependent; only the state topology (single sustained working span, no waiting, one process_exited) is asserted and is robust regardless of flush count.

## Recipe-shape note (SCRIPT / interactive)

Recorded with the INTERACTIVE pi driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh, a tmux REPL) rather than headless `pi --print`. WHY (concern C6): headless `pi --print` exits the instant it emits the final reply, so the daemon's scanner can miss the terminal working→ready settle before process_exited; script-mode keeps pi alive across one send + wait_turn so the daemon observes the settle cleanly, then a single tmux teardown yields one clean process_exited. The prompt deliberately elicits a LONGER reply (a 6-line haiku, one line at a time) so multiple assistant flushes stream before the turn-end stop. C7 / SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch (it bridges the gap until the UUID-keyed rollout transcript appears), so session_birth — which only asserts 'a session appears in ready within 2s of launch' — legitimately matches that pre-session. The streaming turn arc, pid_bind and teardown all pin to the rollout session via the first-working anchor (NO session pin on turn_start, so the matcher binds the FIRST working, necessarily the rollout session; the pre-session never goes working) + same_session_as. The phase DSL has no per-flush form, so the no-flicker property is asserted as a single sustained working span: exactly ONE working→ready arc with a no-state_transition-to-ready invariant across the streaming window, plus a no-waiting invariant.
caveats: RECONCILED 2026-05-28 (consistency-gate): daemon_capability full→incapable. A live recording proved pi writes each completed message as ONE consolidated rollout line (stopReason=stop) with NO intermediate partial-assistant flushes — incremental streaming is terminal-UI only and never reaches the JSONL the daemon tails. The canonical streaming-partial-writes signal (state held across multiple partial flushes) therefore leaves no observable trace. Viewer now renders orange/unobservable; scenarios.json by_adapter.pi applicable:false agrees.

The whole streamed turn is a SINGLE working→ready arc: pi sustains working across every partial/streamed assistant flush — only the final assistant message with stopReason=stop ends the turn. The spec asserts exactly one turn_end ready, no intermediate ready flap (streaming-window invariant), no waiting.

Pi maps a mid-stream `compaction` top-level event to assistant (sustains working), so the stale coverage caveat 'compaction event interspersing risks flicker' does not hold — this is why the verdict is reassessed to yes/yes rather than the coverage cell's irrlicht_observes:partial.
Pi cannot accept a pre-assigned UUID — it generates one itself; the interactive driver discovers the UUID from the session header on line 1 of the freshly-created rollout transcript.

Because the live REPL bridges with a transient proc-<PID> pre-session, the turn phase anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher binds the FIRST working (necessarily the rollout session; the pre-session never goes working); every later phase pins to it via same_session_as.

Confidence 0.72: a live CLI cannot be forced to emit a torn/partial JSONL line on demand (that is the oversized-transcript-line cell); the property asserted is state stability across streamed content, and the exact flush count is model-dependent — only the state topology is asserted.

sources.note: parser.go:78-83 — assistant message with stopReason==stop → turn_done (working→ready); EVERY other assistant message (incl. streamed/partial flushes) → assistant (mid-turn), sustains working, excluded from IsAgentDone(). parser.go:55-60 — top-level `compaction` event → assistant (sustains working), so a mid-stream compaction cannot flicker to ready.

Sibling pi cell confirming the parser sustains working across intermediate assistant transcript writes and settles only on stopReason=stop; events arc is ready→working→ready with no waiting transition.
streaming-partial-writes: state stays working across all partial flushes — no flicker to ready before the turn-end signal arrives; final state ready.
Interactive tmux REPL driver; supports send / wait_turn / interrupt / sleep, keeps the rollout alive so the daemon observes the working→ready settle before teardown (concern C6).
One pi turn = a single assistant line with stopReason=stop; no intermediate partial-assistant flushes — confirms pi writes completed messages atomically (partial-flush streaming is unobservable).

recipe.notes: DEGRADED to applicable:false after a live recording proved the streaming-stability property is not observable for pi at the transcript layer. SCOPE_NOTE: pi writes its rollout JSONL session file ONCE, on turn completion — the assistant turn lands as a SINGLE consolidated `message`/assistant line carrying `stopReason=stop` and the whole multi-line reply in one content block (confirmed: a '6-line poem, one line at a time' prompt produced exactly 1 user line + 1 assistant(stop=stop) line). There are ZERO intermediate partial-assistant flushes in the transcript the daemon tails; pi's incremental streaming is purely a terminal-UI rendering concern that never reaches the JSONL. The spec's streaming window — sustaining `working` across multiple non-terminal partial flushes with no premature `→ ready` — therefore has nothing to assert over: the completed turn lands all-at-once, so the daemon emits ready→working→ready essentially instantaneously (working→ready at +0 ms because stopReason=stop is already present on the first activity). The live recording also exhibited a SECOND spurious working→ready arc ~2 s later from the daemon re-touching the already-completed file — a file-touch artifact, NOT streamed-content flicker, and unrelated to the property this cell targets. Validator result on the captured recording: 1/5 phases (turn_start 'no state_transition to ready' invariant violated at +0 ms). This is an adapter-transport limitation, not a flaky recipe: no live-CLI prompt can make pi emit intermediate partial-assistant JSONL flushes. The torn/partial-line concern lives in the separate oversized-transcript-line cell. expected.jsonl is retained as the documented target should pi ever switch to incremental rollout writes. Reassessment was yes/yes (0.72) on the PARSER logic (parser.go:78-83 sustains working on every non-stop assistant message; parser.go:55-60 maps compaction to assistant so even a mid-stream compaction can't flicker) — the parser would hold working correctly IF partial flushes existed, but pi's transport never produces them, so the cell is unrecordable as specified.
